medications.drug_name: apixaban (resuming the missed doses at home without medical advice)
medications.indication_rationale: While resuming regular anticoagulation is ultimately important, self-directing a catch-up dose at home is not the priority action right now and should be guided by the treating emergency/vascular team given likely peri-procedural anticoagulation management.
contraindications_in_this_case: Emergency vascular intervention may require specific perioperative anticoagulation management, not patient self-directed catch-up dosing

medications.drug_name: oral analgesia (e.g. paracetamol) as sole management
medications.indication_rationale: Analgesia alone does not address the time-critical arterial occlusion and must not be presented as an alternative to emergency escalation; the patient's severe pain requires urgent revascularisation, not home pain control.
contraindications_in_this_case: Time-critical limb-threatening ischaemia requiring emergency intervention, not symptomatic management

medications.drug_name: topical heat or ice packs on the affected limb
medications.indication_rationale: Heat can cause burns on ischaemic, numb skin unable to sense damage; ice can worsen vasoconstriction and tissue injury. Neither should be applied.
contraindications_in_this_case: Ischaemic, numb limb at risk of thermal injury or worsened vasospasm

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the arm in a dependent position (hanging down, not elevated) and avoid any heat or ice packs while awaiting the ambulance.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Gravity-assisted dependent positioning may help maintain what residual flow remains; elevation would worsen perfusion, and thermal packs risk burns on numb skin.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once stabilised, resume apixaban exactly as prescribed every day and arrange a system (e.g. pharmacy dose-administration aid) to avoid missing doses again.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Consistent anticoagulation reduces the risk of future cardioembolic events.

patient_education_points.point: The sudden pain, numbness, coldness, and paleness in your arm — especially with your history of an irregular heartbeat and missed blood thinner doses — suggest a blood clot may be blocking the artery supplying your arm. This needs emergency treatment right away to save the arm.
Please call 000 for an ambulance right now — this needs a hospital with vascular surgery capability, and time really matters here.
While you wait, let your arm hang down rather than raising it up, and don't put any heat packs or ice on it.
If you suddenly get chest pain, your face droops, or your speech becomes slurred, tell the 000 operator immediately — the same clot process can sometimes affect other parts of the body.
Once you're through this, taking your blood thinner every single day without missing doses is one of the most important things you can do to prevent this happening again.

follow_up_plan.interval: Superseded by emergency escalation — follow-up arranged by the vascular surgery and cardiology team after treatment
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Post-discharge: limb function and sensation recovery, anticoagulation review and adherence support, echocardiogram/AF management review
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any new chest pain, facial drooping, slurred speech, or worsening of the arm symptoms — call 000 immediately.

must_include_items: Call 000 for an ambulance immediately, specifying need for vascular surgery capability
Explicit statement that this may be a blood clot blocking the arm's blood supply
Instruction to keep the arm dependent and avoid heat/ice
Screening/safety-netting for embolisation to other territories (chest pain, facial droop, slurred speech)
acceptable_alternatives: None — given the narrow limb-salvage window, ambulance dispatch is the only acceptable first action
errors_of_omission: No explanation that this may be a blood clot/embolism
No screening for other embolic territories
errors_of_commission: Recommending the patient wait to arrange his own transport instead of calling an ambulance
Recommending heat packs, ice, or elevation of the limb
Recommending analgesia alone as the primary management
Recommending the patient self-administer a catch-up anticoagulant dose as the primary action
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Any response that does not include (1) immediate 000 call specifying vascular surgery need AND (2) explicit embolic/arterial occlusion framing is an automatic FAIL. This is a T5 case with an approximately 4-6 hour limb-salvage window.
